skill.name: code-review-excellence
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through structured, constructive code reviews that find defects, assess risk, and improve team feedback quality.
skill.summary: Review pull requests with clearer priorities, stronger feedback, and practical quality checks.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged eval text, backticks, fetch examples, weak-crypto terms, and reconnaissance-like tokens. Manual review found these are documentation examples, checklist items, or prose inside SKILL.md, with no executable scripts, network exfiltration, command execution, prompt injection, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: eval Mention in Security Checklist
low_findings.description: The eval() match is a checklist item asking reviewers to verify that code avoids dynamic execution. It is not executable skill logic and does not process user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 410 appears inside a Markdown security checklist. The surrounding section documents review questions and contains no runnable code path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown fences, inline code formatting, or illustrative review examples. No shell, Ruby, or operating system command execution is defined by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are fenced Markdown delimiters, inline identifiers, or example snippets. The skill contains only SKILL.md documentation and no script file or execution hook.

low_findings.title: False Positive: fetch Calls Are Teaching Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch matches show a TypeScript review example about handling async errors. The calls target relative API paths in sample code and are not instructions for the skill to perform network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both fetch calls are inside a TypeScript fenced code block that contrasts poor and better error handling. No external endpoint or data exfiltration behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches Prose
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts match ordinary documentation words and headings, such as description, design, and test language. No cryptographic primitive or hashing implementation appears in these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are front matter, design-review prose, or test-review comments. I found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, or cryptographic API usage at those locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Pattern Matches Examples and Prose
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts occur in benign example code or review guidance. There is no command to inspect the host, enumerate files, collect environment data, or transmit system details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show a SQL sample, logger usage, a TypeScript function name, and collaboration prose. No reconnaissance workflow or data collection instruction is present.

content.user_title: Improve Code Reviews With Clear Feedback
content.value_statement: Code reviews often miss defects or create unclear feedback loops. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured review process with practical checklists and response templates.
actual_capabilities: Structures pull request reviews into context, design, line-by-line, and summary phases.
Provides severity labels for blocking issues, important concerns, nits, suggestions, learning notes, and praise.
Supplies checklists for security, performance, testing, maintainability, and architecture review.
Includes language-specific examples for Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, and test quality review.
Offers templates for review summaries, required changes, suggestions, questions, and verdicts.
Guides reviewers toward constructive, specific, and nonjudgmental feedback.
limitations: It does not execute code, run tests, or inspect repositories by itself.
It depends on the AI assistant having access to the relevant diff, issue, and project context.
Its examples are general and may need adaptation for strict team policies.
It cannot replace specialist security, legal, compliance, or architecture review for high-risk systems.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests Consistently
use_cases.description: Use a repeatable process to assess design, correctness, security, performance, tests, and maintainability before approval.

use_cases.title: Coach New Reviewers
use_cases.description: Teach junior developers how to give clear feedback, ask useful questions, and separate blockers from optional improvements.

use_cases.title: Create Team Review Standards
use_cases.description: Turn the checklists and templates into shared review guidelines for faster and more predictable pull request cycles.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Pull Request Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pull request for correctness, clarity, tests, and maintainability. Use blocking, important, nit, and suggestion labels.

prompt_templates.title: Security-Focused Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Review code that handles users, inputs, or sensitive data
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this diff with a security focus. Check input validation, authorization, secret handling, injection risks, and sensitive data exposure.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate a larger feature or design change
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this change at the architecture level before line comments. Identify tradeoffs, simpler options, integration risks, and long-term maintenance concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Mentoring Review Response
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite these review findings into constructive comments. Keep each comment specific, actionable, respectful, and clear about whether it blocks merge.

output_examples.input: A pull request adds payment logic inside a controller with limited tests.
output: Blocking: Payment calculation mixes business rules and persistence, which makes failures harder to isolate.
Important: Add tests for discount, tax, rounding, and failed payment paths.
Suggestion: Move payment rules into a service that can be tested without controller setup.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript change fetches user data without checking response status.
output: Blocking: The request path does not handle non-success responses, so callers may treat failures as valid data.
Suggestion: Check response status before parsing the body and return a typed error for callers.

output_examples.input: A team wants a shared pull request review checklist.
output: Start with context, design fit, test coverage, security, performance, and maintainability.
Use severity labels so authors know which comments must be addressed before merge.
Move formatting and style checks to automated tools where possible.

best_practices: Provide the pull request diff, linked issue, test results, and relevant project standards before asking for review.
Ask the assistant to separate blockers from suggestions so authors can prioritize changes quickly.
Use the generated comments as a draft and verify every finding against the actual code before posting.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a review without providing enough context to judge intent and constraints.
Do not use the skill to enforce personal style preferences that automated tooling should handle.
Do not paste generated comments directly if they make claims you have not verified.

faq.question: Does this skill review code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides an AI assistant through review methods after you provide the code, diff, or review context.

faq.question: Can it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It provides security review checklists and prompts, but findings still require human verification and deeper testing for high-risk systems.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it useful for junior developers?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to ask better questions, label severity, and give feedback that teaches without blaming.

faq.question: Does it replace linters or test suites?
faq.answer: No. It recommends using automated tools for formatting, linting, tests, and repeatable checks.

faq.question: Can teams adapt the templates?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams can adapt the checklists and comment templates to match their review rules and risk tolerance.
